elements.sent-2411-001: Fractal geometries, characterized by self-similar patterns and non-integer dimensions, provide an intriguing platform for exploring topological phases of matter.
elements.sent-2411-002: In this work, we introduce a theoretical framework that leverages isospectral reduction to effectively simplify complex fractal structures, revealing the presence of topologically protected boundary and corner states.
elements.sent-2411-003: Our approach demonstrates that fractals can support topological phases, even in the absence of traditional driving mechanisms such as magnetic fields or spin-orbit coupling.
elements.sent-2411-004: The isospectral reduction not only elucidates the underlying topological features but also makes this framework broadly applicable to a variety of fractal systems.
elements.sent-2411-005: Furthermore, our findings suggest that these topological phases may naturally occur in materials with fractal structures found in nature.
elements.sent-2411-006: This work opens new avenues for designing fractal-based topological materials, advancing both theoretical understanding and experimental exploration of topology in complex, self-similar geometries.
elements.sent-2411-007: Symmetry-protected topological phases of matter have proved to be an important theme in condensed-matter physics.
elements.sent-2411-008: They provide a robust framework that allows for the characterization of a system based solely on the symmetries that are present.
elements.sent-2411-009: The exploration of these novel phases of matter started with the discovery of the quantum Hall effect, where the topology is driven by magnetic field, breaking time-reversal symmetry \cite{klitzing_new_1980, haldane_model_1988 .}
elements.sent-2411-010: This was followed by the discovery of the quantum spin Hall effect, where the magnetic field is replaced by the spin-orbit coupling, which preserves time-reversal symmetry \cite{kane_quantum_2005, kane_z_2005 .}
elements.sent-2411-011: Moreover, strain or curvature can be used to induce the quantum valley Hall effect because strain and curvature produce pseudo-magnetic fields on the different valley degrees of freedom \cite{xiao_valley-contrasting_2007, levy_strain-induced_2010 .}
elements.sent-2411-012: A significant development was the realization that crystalline symmetries can also be leveraged to protect topological phases, yielding topological crystalline insulators and, consequently, crystalline higher-order topological insulators (HOTIs) \cite{fu_topological_2007, fang_new_2015, slager_space_2013, van_miert_higher-order_2018, benalcazar_electric_2017,schindler_fractional_2019 .}
elements.sent-2411-013: The latter system in $D$ dimensions does not exhibit robust modes on its $(D-1)$-dimensional boundary, but instead hosts modes on its $(D-d)$-dimensional boundaries, where $d \geq 2$ is the order of the HOTI.
elements.sent-2411-014: Therefore, there is a plethora of different mechanisms that can drive topology.
elements.sent-2411-015: In the quantum Hall systems, an (effective) magnetic field drives topology in different sectors of the Hamiltonian.
elements.sent-2411-016: By imposing additional crystalline symmetries, the quantum Hall-type phases can be extended from two to three dimensions to obtain a second-order 3-dimensional HOTI, where combined crystal and time-reversal symmetry protect the topological phase.
elements.sent-2411-017: An example of this is topology protected by a mirror-graded Chern number, which predicts hinge states \cite{schindler_higher-order_2018 .}
elements.sent-2411-018: Other types of HOTIs are systems in obstructed atomic limits \cite{bradlyn_topological_2017 and systems with a quantized multipole moment \cite{benalcazar_electric_2017 , which have $0-$dimensional corner states and are $D-$th order HOTIs in $D$ dimensions.}}
elements.sent-2411-019: In both of these classes, the topology is driven by a staggering (or `breathing') of the hopping parameters.
elements.sent-2411-020: Fractals, characterized by self-similarity and non-integer dimensions, have recently emerged as a new frontier in the study of topological systems \cite{canyellas_topological_2024, conte_fractal-lattice_2024, lage_quasi-one-dimensional_2023, manna_noncrystalline_2022, manna_higher-order_2022, osseweijer_haldane_2024, li_fractality-induced_2023, kempkes_design_2019 .}
elements.sent-2411-021: In particular, fractal geometries like the Sierpiński carpet and gasket have demonstrated their capacity to host robust, topologically protected modes.
elements.sent-2411-022: Nevertheless, these studies have typically involved systems that are already topologically non-trivial before being placed onto a fractal lattice.
elements.sent-2411-023: Here, we propose a fundamentally different mechanism by which fractality itself can induce topological phases of matter.
elements.sent-2411-024: Our work explores how the self-similar nature of fractal unit cells can lead to the emergence of topological corner states, even when there are no explicit magnetic field, spin-orbit coupling, or staggering in the hopping parameters.
elements.sent-2411-025: This suggests that fractality itself can serve as a driving mechanism for topological behavior, opening new avenues for the exploration of topological phases in novel geometries.
elements.figure-2411-001: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{Fig1_new.png} \caption{(a) DOS for a kagome lattice system (i.e. without fractal geometry). The inset shows the bulk lattice (b) DOS for the Sierpiński-fractal unit cell structure. The blue circles highlight in-gap modes, and the inset shows the bulk lattice structure. (c) Spatial distribution of the topologically protected corner modes, indicated by the second blue circle in (b). (d) The isospectral reduction of a first-generation Sierpínski unit cell. Red sites indicate the set $S$, white sites denote $\overline{S}$. The resulting unit cell is that of a breahting kagome lattice with intracell hopping $v(E)$ and onsite potential $a(E)$. The energy is measured in units of $t$.} \label{fig:DOS}
elements.sent-2411-026: We consider the simplest possible tight-binding Hamiltonians of the form \begin{equation}H = t\sum_{\langle ij \rangle \in \Lambda} c^\dagger_i c^{}_j, \label{eq:H}\end{equation} i.e. a particle on a lattice $\Lambda$ with only nearest-neighbour hopping $t$.
elements.sent-2411-027: As a first example, we consider the kagome lattice \cite{kempkes_robust_2019 , for which we show in \cref{fig:DOS (a) the density of states (DOS) as a function of energy $E$, which is measured in units of $t$.}}
elements.sent-2411-028: The inset shows the corresponding bulk lattice structure.
elements.sent-2411-029: In the absence of staggered hopping, the kagome Hamiltonian has a gapless spectrum, with a flat band at $E=-2t$, at which the DOS diverges, and a Dirac point at $E=t$.
elements.sent-2411-030: Breathing versions of the kagome lattice have been extensively studied, yielding corner modes and fractionalized corner charges \cite{kempkes_robust_2019, kunst_lattice_2018, benalcazar_quantization_2019 .}
elements.sent-2411-031: Nevertheless, in the absence of breathing, the kagome lattice is (semi)-metallic and does not host topological modes.
elements.sent-2411-032: Figure~\ref{fig:DOS (b) depicts the DOS of a lattice built from unit cells comprised of first-generation Sierpínski fractals, as shown in the inset.}
elements.sent-2411-033: From now on, we will refer to this lattice as a ``Sierpínski-kagome lattice'', owing to it being constructed by replacing the triangular structure in a kagome lattice by a Sierpínski fractal structure.
elements.sent-2411-034: The DOS depicts multiple flat bands, but more importantly, reveals that the spectrum is gapped for certain filling factors.
elements.sent-2411-035: Since the Hamiltonian in \cref{eq:H only contains one energy scale, the hopping $t$, the existence of bandgaps in the fractal lattice can not be attributed to the Hamiltonian itself.}
elements.sent-2411-036: Instead, the bandgaps are a consequence of the introduction of holes in the lattice.
elements.sent-2411-037: Moreover, two gaps host in-gap states, indicated by blue circles in \cref{fig:DOS (b).}
elements.sent-2411-038: The spatial profile of the states corresponding to the right circle is displayed in \cref{fig:DOS (c), clearly revealing localized corner states.. Self-similarity.}
elements.sent-2411-039: The spectral features of a system inherit the self-similar nature of the fractal unit cells from which it is composed.
elements.sent-2411-040: To quantitatively describe this behavior, we introduce the concept of isospectral reduction (ISR) \cite{bunimovich_isospectral_2014 .}
elements.sent-2411-041: The ISR is obtained by considering the partitioning of a matrix (or graph) into a set $S$ and $\overline{S}$, and then reducing the original problem to an effective problem on the subset $S$.
elements.sent-2411-042: Formally, the ISR of a matrix $H$ is given by \begin{equation}\mathcal{R}_S(H,E) = H_{SS} - H_{S\overline{S}}\left( H_{\overline{SS}} - E\mathbb{I} \right)^{-1} H_{\overline{S}S}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-043: It converts the linear eigenvalue problem $H\boldsymbol{\psi = E\boldsymbol{\psi}$ into a nonlinear one $\mathcal{R _S(H,E)\boldsymbol{\psi _S = E\boldsymbol{\psi _S$, where $\boldsymbol{\psi _S$ is the $S$ component of $\boldsymbol{\psi}$.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-044: Importantly, performing an ISR does not forego spectral information of $H$.
elements.sent-2411-045: Recently, the ISR has been used in the context of latent symmetries to design flat-band lattices \cite{morfonios_flat_2021 and to explain ``accidental degeneracies'' \cite{rontgen_latent_2021 .}}
elements.sent-2411-046: Moreover, latent symmetries were also used to construct latent versions of the Su-Schrieffer-Heeger (SSH) model \cite{rontgen_latent_2023 and of the non-Hermitian SSH model \cite{eek_emergent_2024 , and to build higher-order topological crystalline insulators protected by latent crystalline symmetries \cite{eek_higher-order_2024 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-047: For fractal lattices, the ISR can be used to relate lattices of different generations to each other.
elements.sent-2411-048: A similar approach has been applied before \cite{domany_solutions_1983, kempkes_design_2019 , but did not touch upon the presence of topological states.}
elements.sent-2411-049: In the case of the first-generation Sierpínski-kagome lattice, we take the sites indicated in red in \cref{fig:DOS (d) to be the set $S$.}
elements.sent-2411-050: The remainder of the sites form $\overline{S}$.
elements.sent-2411-051: If one then performs the ISR, $\mathcal{R _S(H,E)$ takes the form of a breathing kagome Hamiltonian with intercell hopping $1$, intracell hopping $v(E) = E/\left[(E^2 -1)(E-2) \right]$, and onsite potential $a(E)= 2(E^2-E-1)/\left[(E^2 -1)(E-2) \right]$.}
elements.sent-2411-052: Consequently, modes at different energies are subject to an effective Hamiltonian with different parameters.
elements.sent-2411-053: The effective system resultant from performing the ISR on the first-generation Sierpínski-kagome lattice is depicted in \cref{fig:DOS (d).}
elements.sent-2411-054: For $v(E)\neq 1$, this effective kagome Hamiltonian breathes and may drive higher-order topology.
elements.sent-2411-055: In the SM we present similar equations relating $n$-th generation lattices to $(n-1)$-th generation lattices.
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{Fig2_new.png} \caption{Spatial distribution of corner state wave-functions on fractal lattices. The heat map denotes the summed $|\psi|^2$ of a set of topological corner states. Top line of the figure shows how the fractals, from which the lattices are obtained, can be iteratively generated. (a) Fourth-generation fractal flake based on the construction method employed to obtain the fractal lattice in \cref{fig:DOS}(b-d). (b) Third-generation lattice obtained from an alternate construction method to (a), as can be seen at the top of the figure. This same method was employed in \cite{kempkes_design_2019, osseweijer_haldane_2024}. (c) Third-generation fractal lattice based on the Pascal triangle mod $3$. (d) Third-generation lattice construction based on the hexaflake fractal. The lattice is obtained by putting seven sites in each polygon that composes the fractal.} \label{fig:fractals}
elements.sent-2411-056: The systems treated in this work exhibit a rotational symmetry.
elements.sent-2411-057: On the level of the Bloch Hamiltonian, $n$-fold rotations are represented by $\hat{C _n h(\kk) \hat{C _n^{-1 = h(D_{C_n \kk)$, where $\hat{C _n$ rotates the sites in the unit cell by $2\pi/n$ radians and $D_{C_n}$ rotates the momentum $\kk$ by the same amount.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-058: The higher-order topology of such systems is characterized in terms of rotational invariants, introduced in Ref.~\cite{benalcazar_quantization_2019 .}
elements.sent-2411-059: The Sierpínski-kagome Hamiltonian satisfies a $C_3$-symmetry and is therefore characterized by two integers $\chi^{(3) \equiv ([K_1^{(3) ], [K_2^{(3) ])$, which are obtained from the symmetry eigenvalues of the Bloch-functions at the high-symmetry point $\mathbf{K}$ (see SM).}}}
elements.sent-2411-060: When $\chi^{(3) \neq \mathbf{0}$, the system is in an obstructed atomic limit, characterized by a finite bulk dipole moment and/or fractional corner charge.}
elements.sent-2411-061: For the set of states highlighted by a blue circle in \cref{fig:DOS (b), we have $\chi^{(3) = (1,0)$, indicating that they are topological.}}
elements.sent-2411-062: The self-similarity of the fractal lattices allows for a second approach to obtain the topological phase of the system.
elements.sent-2411-063: Since repeated applications of the ISR relate the fractal lattice to an effective breathing kagome lattice, one can use properties of the latter.
elements.sent-2411-064: In order to do so, we must first obtain the corner-state energies.
elements.sent-2411-065: For the breathing kagome lattice, the corner modes sit at $E_c=0$ (onsite potential is zero for the kagome lattice).
elements.sent-2411-066: For the effective kagome model, this yields the condition for corner mode energy $E_c=a(E_c)$, where $a(E)$ is the effective onsite potential.
elements.sent-2411-067: Next, one must check whether this state is topological.
elements.sent-2411-068: The breathing kagome model is topological if the intracell hopping is smaller in magnitude than the intercell hopping.
elements.sent-2411-069: Hence, for the effective model we require $|v(E_c)|<1$.
elements.sent-2411-070: If this requirement holds for a solution to $E_c=a(E_c)$, then there will be topological corner modes at that energy $E_c$.
elements.sent-2411-072: There is an abundance of novel states on fractal lattices.
elements.sent-2411-073: For instance, Refs.~\cite{conte_fractal-lattice_2024, biswas_designer_2023 treat the different types of compact localized states (CLS) present on fractals.}
elements.sent-2411-074: Similar states also form on the lattices considered here.
elements.sent-2411-075: Instead, we focus on topological corner-like states.
elements.sent-2411-076: In the previous section, we showed that by successively applying the ISR, the fractal lattices can be mapped to an effective breathing kagome model.
elements.sent-2411-077: An expression for the corner states in the kagome model (which is exact in the thermodynamic limit) was given in Ref.~\cite{kunst_lattice_2018 .}
elements.sent-2411-078: Since the eigenstates of the ISR are equal to the full eigenstates of $H$ projected on $S$ [i.e. $\mathcal{R _S(H,E) \psi_S = E \psi_S$], based on Ref.~\cite{kunst_lattice_2018 we obtain \begin{equation}\ket{\Psi_\text{corner}}_S = \mathcal{N} \sum_{m,m'} \left[- v(E_c) \right]^{m+m'} c^\dagger_{A,m,m'} \ket{0}.\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-079: Here, $m$ and $m'$ are the unit cell indices in the $\mathbf{a _1$ $=(1,0)^T$ and $\mathbf{a _2$ $=(1/2,\sqrt{3 /2)^T$ directions, respectively.}}}
elements.sent-2411-080: For clarity, $m$ and $m'$ have been indicated in \cref{fig:DOS (c).}
elements.sent-2411-081: Furthermore, $A$ indicates the $A$-sublattice of the kagome Hamiltonian, i.e. the bottom left site in \cref{fig:DOS (d).}
elements.sent-2411-082: The solution can be extended to $\overline{S}$ by realizing that $\ket{\psi _S$ and $\ket{\psi _{\overline{S }$ are related: $\ket{\psi _{\overline{S = (E-H_{\overline{SS )^{-1 H_{\overline{S S \ket{\psi _S$, which yields the full solution $\ket{\psi = (\ket{\psi _S,\ket{\psi _{\overline{S )^T$ to $H$.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-083: The last equality is derived in detail in the SM \footnote{We recognize that obtaining the full eigenstates requires a matrix inversion, which is a computationally costly operation.}
elements.sent-2411-084: However, in the case of the kagome type lattices, $H_{\overline{SS }$ is a blockdiagonal matrix with the individual (identical) blocks being $3^{n+1 \times 3^{n+1}$ matrices (here $n$ is the generation of the fractal lattice).}}
elements.sent-2411-086: By rotational symmetry, similar solutions exist for the two other corners.
elements.sent-2411-088: There exist multiple methods for constructing lattices from a Sierpínski gasket.
elements.sent-2411-089: One possibility is to construct a fractal lattice by taking a Sierpínski gasket, putting three sites in each triangle, and then connecting nearest neighbours, see \cref{fig:fractals (a).}
elements.sent-2411-090: Another approach is to put a site in the center and on the corners of each triangle in a gasket and subsequently connecting the nearest neighbours.
elements.sent-2411-091: This approach is shown in \cref{fig:fractals (b), yielding a honeycomb-like lattice.}
elements.sent-2411-092: This version of the Sierpínski was studied in the presence of Haldane-type next-nearest neighbour hopping in Refs.~\cite{osseweijer_haldane_2024, li_fractality-induced_2023 and on an atomic simulator platform in Ref.~\cite{kempkes_design_2019 .}}
elements.sent-2411-093: Nevertheless, neither of these works investigated the higher-order topology of the system.
elements.sent-2411-094: In the SM, we show that, by using a similar procedure as for the system in \cref{fig:fractals (a), this system can be reduced to an effective breathing kagome lattice, elucidating the nature of its topological corner states.}
elements.sent-2411-095: These fractal unit cells can be used to construct flakes that are fractal-like, conform \cref{fig:DOS (b)-(c), or flakes that are a fractal themselves, \cref{fig:fractals .}}
elements.sent-2411-096: Consider constructing a flake out of three unit cells that compose a $n$-th generation Sierpínski-kagome lattice.
elements.sent-2411-097: We obtain a flake that is a generation $(n+1)$ Sierpínski gasket.
elements.sent-2411-098: Figure.~\ref{fig:fractals (a) shows a $4$-th generation Sierpínski gasket, with three topological corner states.}
elements.sent-2411-099: Invoking topological properties is based on a bulk-boundary correspondence (BBC).
elements.sent-2411-100: From a bulk topology perspective, the fractals in \cref{fig:fractals (a) and (b) consist of three unit cells, all three of them at the `boundary' of the system.}
elements.sent-2411-101: Nevertheless, we can still heuristically argue based on a BBC.
elements.sent-2411-102: Since the corner states are exponentially localized, they do not `see' the largest and second-largest holes.
elements.sent-2411-103: Consequently, these holes could be filled and the corner states would still be solutions of the lattice.
elements.sent-2411-104: The `filled system' is similar to the one considered in Figs.~\ref{fig:DOS (b)-(c), for which the BBC holds.}
elements.sent-2411-105: The caveat here is that the localization length $\ell$ of a corner state should be smaller than the distance from the corner to a large hole.
elements.sent-2411-106: Since $\ell$ is inversely proportional to the gap in which the state sits, the corner states in large gaps survive on a fractal flake.
elements.sent-2411-107: The presented analysis does not restrain itself solely to lattices based on the Sierpínski gasket.
elements.sent-2411-108: A direct generalization can be made by considering the Pascal triangle mod $k$ fractal, where $k$ is a prime.
elements.sent-2411-109: This fractal is iteratively constructed in a similar manner to the Sierpínski gasket, not by taking 2 copies as a side of the next generation, but taking $k$ copies \footnote{Another way to view this: The Sierpínski triangle is constructed by taking 3 identical copies of generation $n$ to construct generation $n+1$.}
elements.sent-2411-110: Meanwhile for a Pascal triangle mod $k$, one has to take $k(k+1)/2$ copies of generation $n$ to construct $n+1$.. .
elements.sent-2411-111: This makes the Sierpínski gasket a special case: it is a Pascal triangle mod $2$.
elements.sent-2411-112: The Pascal triangle family of fractals has a Hausdorff dimension given by $d_H = \log_k\left[ k(k+1)/2\right]$.
elements.sent-2411-113: Figure~\ref{fig:fractals (c) shows a lattice constructed from a $3$-rd generation Pascal triangle mod $3$, hosting topological corner modes.}
elements.sent-2411-114: The three aforementioned lattices all have a $C_3$-symmetry and, through the ISR, can be related to an effective breathing kagome lattice.
elements.sent-2411-115: This does not mean that topology induced by fractality is constrained to systems that reduce to a kagome lattice.
elements.sent-2411-116: On the contrary, it is applicable to a wide range of systems.
elements.sent-2411-117: Figure~\ref{fig:fractals (d) displays a lattice based on the hexaflake fractal, which has $d_H = \log 7/ \log 3 \approx 1.77$.}
elements.sent-2411-118: Its boundary is a Koch curve, and its interior contains Koch snowflakes.
elements.sent-2411-119: The hexaflake has a $6$-fold rotation symmetry, such that its topology should be characterized by the $\chi^{(6)}$ invariant \cite{benalcazar_quantization_2019 .}
elements.sent-2411-120: In the SM, we show how through the ISR the hexaflake lattice can be reduced to the Kekulé lattice, a breathing honeycomb lattice.
elements.sent-2411-121: This system is known to exhibit topology, explaining the topological corner states displayed in \cref{fig:fractals (d) \cite{freeney_edge-dependent_2020 .}}
elements.sent-2411-122: Detailed calculations for all fractals in \cref{fig:fractals are performed in the SM.}
elements.figure-2411-003: \includegraphics[width=\linewidth]{Fig4.png} \caption{Spatial distribution of corner state wave-functions. The heat map denotes the summed $|\psi|^2$ of a set of topological corner states. (a) Fourth-generation Viczek ($4$-flake) fractal, the lattice is obtained by replacing the squares directly by sites. (b) Third-generation pentaflake ($5$-flake) fractal, an example of a $5$-fold symmetric fractal structure. (c) Fourth-generation Sierpínski tetrahedron fractal. The lattice is obtained by placing four sites in each tetrahedron in a similar way to the Sierpínski gasket presented in \cref{fig:fractals}(a). The size of the red balls denotes the amplitude of the wave-function on that given site. (d) Small triangular flake of $[4]$triangulene, in this case $|\psi|$ of the corner states is represented by the heatmap instead of $|\psi|^2$. This way its better visible that the wave-function exponentially decays into the interior and is not (compactly) localized on the outer cells.} \label{fig:outlook}
elements.sent-2411-123: We demonstrated how fractality can be a driving mechanism for higher-order topological phases of matter.
elements.sent-2411-124: By considering lattices where the unit cell was constructed from a $n$-th generation fractal.
elements.sent-2411-125: The kagome lattice is a natural example, as its unit cell could be seen as a zeroth-generation Sierpínski gasket.
elements.sent-2411-126: In \cref{fig:DOS (b) and \cref{fig:fractals (a), we showed how taking a first- and third-generation Sierpínski gasket as the unit cell leads to the emergence of topological corner states.}}
elements.sent-2411-127: The analysis was then extended to different types of lattices, based on different fractals, such as the family of Pascal triangle fractals and the hexaflake.
elements.sent-2411-128: The hexaflake itself is part of a larger family of fractals, namely the $N$-flakes.
elements.sent-2411-129: These are constructed in a similary manner to the hexaflake, but by taking an $N$-sided polygon instead of a hexagon, and surrounding one central polygon by $N$ copies of itself to obtain the next generation \footnote{a variation of the $N$-flake leaves out the central polygon .}
elements.sent-2411-130: A special example of this is the $4$-flake or the Viczek fractal, which is depicted in \cref{fig:outlook (a).}
elements.sent-2411-131: Tight-binding Hamiltonians based on the Viczek fractal move away from being a lattice and tend to look more like `trees'.
elements.sent-2411-133: Furthermore, by employing the ISR, different generations of the Viczek fractal can be related, introducing an effective breathing hopping structure after the ISR.
elements.sent-2411-134: Larger lattices constructed from lower generation Viczek fractals show the same behavior.
elements.sent-2411-135: Indeed, $\mathbb{R ^2$ can only be tiled periodically by systems with a $2$-, $3$-, $4$-, or $6$-fold rotation symmetry.}
elements.sent-2411-136: In \cref{fig:outlook (b), we present an example of this with the pentaflake ($5$-flake), displaying exponentially localized states on each of its corners.}
elements.sent-2411-137: Moreover, the analysis presented in this work does not restrain itself to two spatial dimensions: the Sierpínski tetrahedron is the 3-dimensional counterpart of the gasket, from which we can construct a lattice by placing 4 sites in each tetrahedron and connecting nearest neighbours, see \cref{fig:outlook (c).}
elements.sent-2411-138: The reduction scheme here is identical to the one proposed in \cref{fig:DOS (d), but we reduce onto a tetrahedron.}
elements.sent-2411-139: Repeated application of the ISR maps the Sierpínski-tetrahedron lattice into an effective breathing pyrochlore lattice, which has been shown to host topological corner modes \cite{ezawa_higher-order_2018 .}
elements.sent-2411-140: Fractal, or fractal-like, structures are good candidates to explore novel quantum phases of matter.
elements.sent-2411-141: Even though true fractal objects only exist in the reigns of mathematical theory, finite generation fractal structures are pervasive in nature.
elements.sent-2411-142: One could think of a Lieb lattice, characteristic of cuprate perovskites, as a lattice build from first-generation Sierpínski carpets \cite{lieb_two_1989 .}
elements.sent-2411-143: More recently, the band structures of metal-organic frameworks, highly tunable building blocks for complex lattices, have been analysed \cite{kumar_two-dimensional_2018 .}
elements.sent-2411-144: The versatility of such systems makes them a suitable candidate to explore fractal topology.
elements.sent-2411-145: Honeycomb structures formed by HgTe or CdSe nanocrystals \cite{beugeling_topological_2015 also offer a promising platform to investigate fractal behaviour.}
elements.sent-2411-146: Alternatively, triangulene, a honeycomb structure with its sites replaced by graphene nanoplatelets, presents itself as another suitable candidate \cite{ortiz_theory_2023, catarina_broken-symmetry_2023, turco_magnetic_2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-147: Figure~\ref{fig:outlook (d) displays a small flake of $[3]$triangulene, together with corner states.}
elements.sent-2411-148: Saliently, $[3]$triangulene hosts corner states, while $[2]$triangulene does not.
elements.sent-2411-149: This behaviour is explained by the expressions for the effective hopping parameters upon performing the ISR and is further elaborated upon in the SM.. Conclusion.
elements.sent-2411-150: In this work, we have demonstrated that fractal geometries provide a rich platform for the emergence of topological phases, offering a robust framework that is applicable across a broad spectrum of fractal systems.
elements.sent-2411-151: By extending the theory to various fractal structures, such as Sierpiński gaskets, Viczek, pentaflakes, and $N$-flakes, we have shown that the interplay between fractality and topology is not only theoretically intriguing, but also potentially realizable in real-world materials, such as metal-organic frameworks, triangulenes and honeycomb nanocrystals.
elements.sent-2411-152: These findings suggest that fractal-based topological materials could naturally occur in a variety of physical systems, reflecting the self-similar patterns found in nature.
elements.sent-2411-153: The generality of our approach opens up new avenues for experimental exploration, particularly in the study of naturally occurring and artificially designed fractal materials, which may harbor exotic topological states and functionalities.
elements.sent-2411-154: This broad applicability underscores the significance of fractal topology in advancing our understanding of condensed-matter systems and potentially guiding the development of future quantum materials.. Acknowledgements.
